title: Educational roadmap v2 — from the 3rd month of pregnancy to the 1st year of life

nodes.title: Educational roadmap v2
nodes.notes:
# Evidence-first · from the 3rd month of pregnancy to the 1st year of life

**9 core books in 18 months.** Only titles with strong methodological foundations.

## Selection philosophy

Every title had to pass three tests:

1. **The author's credentials** — whether the author conducted their own research in the field they describe, or merely popularizes the work of others.
2. **Quality of evidence** — replicable studies or systematic reviews, not anecdotes or single small studies.
3. **No hidden harm** — whether the book could mislead the reader in a way that affects decisions (alcohol, infant sleep, medical interventions).

## Pace and division within the couple

- **Limit:** max 2 books/month.
- **Division:** Person A → biology/medicine (Mayo, Karp, Mindell, Medina). Person B → psychology/attachment (Stern, Bowlby, Hrdy, Lieberman). **Both, mandatory:** Simkin, Kleiman, Gottman, Machin, Plomin.
- **Rhythm:** once a week, 30 min of shared conversation about what each of you read.

## Closing principle

These 9 books, read carefully, plus the ongoing resources, amount to more parenting knowledge than 95% of parents have. **Do not read more. Read these — carefully, critically, talking with each other.**

children.title: Phase 1 · 3rd–5th month of pregnancy
children.notes:
## Foundation: pregnancy medicine + the biology of fatherhood

An energetic phase. Goal: a solid biological base (what really happens to the mother's body) and — crucially — a parallel base for the father.

The psychobiology of fatherhood is one of the best-documented areas of research of the past decade, something most Polish guidebooks stay silent about.

children.title: Mayo Clinic Guide to a Healthy Pregnancy
children.notes:
**Author:** Mayo Clinic obstetrics team (academic medical center, US News #1 in many rankings).

**Why:** A nameless team of doctors instead of a single "guru." Medically conservative, but updated and based on current guidelines.

**How to read it:** The first 4 chapters together. The rest is a reference — you only come back to the chapters that interest you.

children.title: The Life of Dad — Anna Machin
children.notes:
**Author:** Anna Machin (Oxford SENRG, evolutionary anthropologist, a decade of research in Robin Dunbar's group).

**Why:** The strongest title on the market about the psychobiology of fatherhood — what happens to a man's brain and hormones in the perinatal period.

**How to read it:** Both of you read it, a month apart — the father first, then the mother. This lets her understand what his brain/body will go through.

children.title: ParentData (Emily Oster)
children.notes:
**parentdata.org** — a source for learning how to read studies, **NOT** for final medical decisions.

**Caveat:** Oster systematically leans toward "it's fine" where the literature says "we don't know." Use it as an exercise in thinking about data; verify decisions with PTGiP/Mayo.

children.title: Evidence Based Birth (Rebecca Dekker)
children.notes: **evidencebasedbirth.com** — PhD, RN. Compares obstetric practices against the medical literature. The best source for questions like "do I have to be induced," "does CTG make sense."

children.title: Cochrane Pregnancy and Childbirth
children.notes: **cochranelibrary.com** — the gold standard, systematic reviews of research. You go there when you encounter conflicting advice and want to check the source.

children.title: PTGiP — guidelines
children.notes: **ptgin.pl** — official Polish gynecological-obstetric standards. Clinical specifics.

children.title: Diary of a CEO — episodes with A. Machin / R. Sapolsky
children.notes: Listening to specific episodes before reading the books helps you pinpoint what to look for.

children.title: Phase 2 · 6th–7th month of pregnancy
children.notes:
## Perinatal psychology and the foundation of attachment

The body already says "baby," the parent's identity is forming. This is the moment for foundational reading — with the right critical context, because Bowlby, though canonical, has well-known limitations.

children.title: The Birth of a Mother — Daniel Stern
children.notes:
**Author:** Daniel Stern (Cornell psychiatrist, microanalysis of mother–infant interaction, his own empirical data).

**Why:** Stern combines psychoanalysis with frame-by-frame recordings and microanalysis — this isn't speculation, it's observation. He shows how a woman's identity changes in the perinatal period.

**Mandatory reading for both of you.**

children.title: A Secure Base — John Bowlby
children.notes:
**Author:** John Bowlby (creator of attachment theory, the foundation of the field).

**Why:** You read it to understand where the entire later field came from. **Do not read it as an instruction manual — read it as a history of ideas.**

IMPORTANT caveats — see the node "Critical context for Bowlby."

children.title: Critical context for Bowlby
children.notes:
Bowlby's theory (1950s–60s) is foundational, but it has three limitations that modern science modifies:

1. **Monotropy** — Bowlby claimed that a child has a single "primary attachment figure." Newer research (Schaffer & Emerson, later Hrdy) shows that a child can simultaneously form multiple secure attachments. Important if you plan to share care equally.
2. **WEIRD bias** — the whole theory was built on observations of the Western middle class. Anthropology (Hrdy) shows that in most human cultures children were raised communally, not dyadically.
3. **Neglect of temperament** — part of what Bowlby attributed to the quality of care has a strong innate basis. This dialogue is developed further in Plomin (Phase 6).

children.title: Center on the Developing Child (Harvard)
children.notes: **developingchild.harvard.edu** — the best synthesizer of research on early development in the world. Free articles, videos, short courses.

children.title: Zero to Three
children.notes: **zerotothree.org** — an American non-profit organization, evidence-based materials on development from 0–3 years.

children.title: BBC Radio 4 — All in the Mind (archive)
children.notes: Archive episodes on attachment theory and its current modifications (e.g. interviews with Marinus van IJzendoorn — a researcher of cross-cultural meta-analyses of attachment).

children.title: Phase 3 · 8th–9th month + birth
children.notes:
## Birth, the first weeks, the mother's mental health

A pragmatic phase. No academic reflection, concrete protocols. At the same time: **BEFORE the birth** you need to know what to watch for in yourself after the birth, because there will be no time for it then.

children.title: The Birth Partner — Penny Simkin
children.notes:
**Author:** Penny Simkin (doula, physiotherapist, 40+ years of practice, a classic of the field).

**Why:** The best handbook for a birth companion — physiology, stages, interventions, the partner's role. Practical, non-ideological.

**Mandatory reading, especially for the father.**

children.title: This Isn't What I Expected — Karen Kleiman
children.notes:
**Author:** Karen Kleiman (clinician, founder of The Postpartum Stress Center).

**Why:** Concrete signs of PPD, postpartum anxiety, intrusive thoughts.

**BOTH OF YOU READ IT BEFORE THE BIRTH** — so you know what to watch for, because it also affects fathers (5–10% PPD in men).

children.title: PPD — critical to remember
children.notes:
**PPD and postpartum anxiety are NOT a "lack of character."**

**Biological basis:** a sudden drop in estrogen/progesterone, sleep deprivation, changes in the HPA axis. It affects 10–20% of mothers and 5–10% of fathers (Paulson & Bazemore, JAMA).

**Risk factors:** previous episodes of depression/anxiety, lack of social support, traumatic birth, feeding problems, a child with a difficult temperament.

**Action BEFORE the birth:** identify specific people to call — a community midwife, gynecologist, perinatal psychiatrist. The number saved in both of your phones.

children.title: KellyMom
children.notes: **kellymom.com** — an English-language knowledge base on feeding with links to research. The most detailed source online.

children.title: Postpartum Support International
children.notes: **postpartum.net** — a helpline, a database of therapists specializing in perinatal care.

children.title: Postpartum Stress Center — course for couples
children.notes: Kleiman runs short courses for couples. Worth the investment of **$50–100** — it saves many weeks of suffering if one of you starts to sink.

children.title: Lactation Science Center (CNoL)
children.notes: The most reliable Polish source on feeding, based on international guidelines.

children.title: Childbirth with Dignity Foundation
children.notes: **rodzicpoludzku.pl** — patients' rights in the perinatal period, a ranking of hospitals in Poland.

children.title: Phase 4 · 0–3 months of life
children.notes:
## The fourth trimester: infant regulation, the couple's relationship

The hardest phase. Reading is short, audiobooks dominate. Only two core titles — both with strong empirical foundations.

**Reading rhythm:** One of you reads, the other manages the chaos. Swap roles every week. Trying to read together will break what is still holding you up — your strength. Audiobooks while feeding, walking, in the shower. 15 min of shared conversation, ideally on a walk with the stroller.

children.title: The Happiest Baby on the Block — Harvey Karp
children.notes:
**Author:** Harvey Karp (pediatrician, USC Keck/UCLA, AAP fellow). RCT support for the 5S (Japan J Nursing Sci 2019).

**IMPORTANT:** Works **only in the first ~3 months** (after the 4th month the calming reflex fades). Karp also has a commercial interest in the Snoo — **don't buy it**, the book is enough.

children.title: And Baby Makes Three — John & Julie Gottman
children.notes:
**Author:** The Gottman Institute (40+ years of longitudinal research on couples).

**Why:** The best book on the couple's relationship after a baby. The statistic that 67% of couples experience a drop in satisfaction has real data behind it.

**Caveat:** The research is mostly on the white American middle class — but the mechanisms are universally sensible.

children.title: AAP HealthyChildren.org
children.notes: Official guidelines of the American Academy of Pediatrics. The fastest reference for "is this normal," "when to see a doctor."

children.title: Possms — Dr Pamela Douglas (NDC)
children.notes: **possms.org** — an Australian pediatrician, the Neuroprotective Developmental Care approach. An alternative to classic sleep training, grounded in neurodevelopment.

children.title: Where Should We Begin? — Esther Perel
children.notes: Not about children — about the couple's relationship. Listen together. Esther Perel: a psychotherapist with 35 years of practice.

children.title: The Gottman Relationship Podcast
children.notes: A direct application of what you're reading in And Baby Makes Three.

children.title: Phase 5 · 4th–7th month of life
children.notes:
## Neurodevelopment, sleep, introducing solids

Energy for reading returns. In this phase, v2 replaced both titles — Siegel went in the bin (the left-brain/right-brain myth), Ferber/Komsta replaced by Mindell.

**A skeptic's strategy on the topic of sleep:** The internet will flood you with "truths." Stick to one principle — if a method promises "nights slept through in 3 days" or "perfect attachment if only," it's marketing, not knowledge. The real development of an infant's sleep is non-monotonic, individual, and prone to regressions.

children.title: Brain Rules for Baby — John Medina
children.notes:
**Author:** John Medina (developmental molecular biologist, affiliate prof. UWashington School of Medicine).

**Why:** A better alternative to Siegel. Medina sticks more strictly to what the research actually shows — he starts with the difference between correlation and causation, and debunks myths (e.g. the Mozart Effect for infants).

children.title: Sleeping Through the Night — Jodi Mindell
children.notes:
**Author:** Jodi Mindell (PhD, director of the Sleep Disorders Center at CHOP — Children's Hospital of Philadelphia).

**Why:** The most balanced book on infant sleep. Mindell publishes peer-reviewed research on sleep. She presents various methods (from no-cry to graduated extinction) with an actual evidence base, without an ideological slant.

children.title: Solid Starts
children.notes: **solidstarts.com** — the most detailed database on introducing solids. Every food with a safety analysis by age (size, texture, choking risk). A team of experts (RD, SLP, MD).

children.title: AAP HealthyChildren — sections on diet and sleep
children.notes: A continuation from Phase 4 — in this phase especially the sections on introducing solids and safe sleep.

children.title: Mindell's Pediatric Sleep Council
children.notes: **babysleep.com** — free materials from one of the world's leading authorities on pediatric sleep.

children.title: Phase 6 · 8th–12th month of life
children.notes:
## Attachment, separation anxiety, the long view

The phase that closes the roadmap and opens the next stage. The final title — Plomin — is deliberately provocative. It is meant to serve as an epistemic shower after everything you've read before.

children.title: Mothers and Others — Sarah Blaffer Hrdy
children.notes:
**Author:** Sarah Blaffer Hrdy (PhD Harvard, evolutionary anthropologist, peer-reviewed book from Harvard University Press).

**Why:** Shows that Homo sapiens evolved as a "cooperative breeder" species — children were raised by the community, not the nuclear couple.

**Value:** An antidote to the pressure of "only mum-and-dad can do this." Help from outside the family is not a failure, it's the evolutionary norm.

children.title: The Emotional Life of the Toddler — Alicia Lieberman
children.notes:
**Author:** Alicia Lieberman (professor at UCSF, pioneer in diagnosing attachment disorders, creator of Child-Parent Psychotherapy — an evidence-based intervention for traumatized young children).

**Why:** A map of the emotional world of a child aged 1–3. The book begins to cover the age after the first year.

children.title: Blueprint — Robert Plomin (the provocation title)
children.notes:
**Author:** Robert Plomin (King's College London, professor of behavioral genetics, 50+ years of twin and adoption studies).

**Why:** Deliberately provocative. Plomin claims (based on huge meta-analyses) that the influence of the "shared environment" (i.e. parenting style) on a child's psychological traits is smaller than we usually assume.

**Caveat:** The book is criticized for genetic determinism and underestimating environmental effects. BUT: as a counterpoint after 18 months of reading about how every parenting decision is incredibly important — a healthy reset.

**The "blocked" status** on this node means: read it ONLY after Bowlby, Stern, Lieberman, and Hrdy. Without those foundations, the counterpoint can mislead you.

children.title: Why Plomin comes at the very end
children.notes:
After 18 months of intensive reading about parenting, there is a risk that Alison Gopnik calls **"carpenter parenting"** — the belief that you can "carve" the right person out of a child by selecting the right methods.

Plomin (and the whole behavioral genetics literature) shows that a large part of a child's traits are innate. This does **NOT** mean that parenting doesn't matter — but it means that your work is not production, only accompanying a being who has already brought their own predispositions.

**Read it critically:** Plomin himself has a tendency to over-interpret in the other direction.

children.title: Center on the Developing Child — stress & resilience
children.notes: Still the best synthesizer. Especially the sections on stress and resilience.

children.title: BBC Reith Lectures (Robert Plomin 2018)
children.notes: A concise introduction to the Blueprint thesis, if the book feels too big.

children.title: Good Inside — Becky Kennedy
children.notes: Clinical psychologist, PhD Columbia. Practical strategies for regulating emotions in the child and in the parent. Strongly oriented toward the second half of the first year and later.

children.title: What absolutely not to read
children.notes:
- **The Wonder Weeks** (van de Rijt & Plooij) — scientifically discredited. Plooij's doctoral student could not replicate the results; Plooij lost his academic position.
- **Babywise** (Ezzo) — a rigid feeding schedule; the AAP issued warnings (a link to failure-to-thrive/malnutrition).
- **The Searses' "The Baby Book"** — a popularization of attachment parenting. Often extrapolates Bowlby toward ideology. Excessive pressure on the mother.
- **Everything by Gabor Maté** — systematically criticized by the medical academy for going beyond the data (ADHD, addiction, trauma).
- **Whole-Brain Child / No-Drama Discipline** (Siegel & Bryson) — the strategies are OK, but the neuroscience is simplified/false (the left-brain/right-brain myth). Medina is better.
- **Any books promising a "perfect" parent/child** — mostly marketing.

children.title: Reading at a distance
children.notes:
Worth knowing, but not as foundations:

- **Emily Oster — Expecting Better / Cribsheet** — learning to read studies is OK, but Oster systematically leans toward downplaying medical warnings. Use it as an exercise, not as an oracle.
- **Ina May Gaskin — Guide to Childbirth** — valuable on the physiology and psychology of natural birth, but skews ideological against medical interventions. Worth knowing, balance it against Simkin and the evidence-based sources.
